questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: What is the name of the part of a cone that remains after cutting off the top with a plane parallel to the base?
questions.hint: It has two circular bases of different sizes.
questions.explanation: A frustum of a cone is the portion between the base and a parallel cutting plane. Option A (cross-section) is just the cut face, not the solid.

source_trace.content_anchor: A frustum of a cone is a part of the cone between the base and a plane parallel to the base

explanations_tiered.definition: A frustum of a cone is the lower part of a cone after cutting off the top parallel to the base.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: It has two circular bases: a larger bottom and a smaller top.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Like a lampshade or a bucket — a cone with its point cut off flat.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The cutting plane is parallel to the base, so both bases are circles.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The frustum's volume can be found by subtracting the small top cone from the original cone.
reasoning_steps: Visualise a cone cut parallel to its base.
The lower part is the frustum.
misconceptions: Cross-section (Option A) is just the cut surface, not the remaining solid.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Frustum of cone = cone with top cut off parallel to base.

glossary_entry.definition: The part of a cone between its base and a plane parallel to the base.

flashcard.front: What is a frustum of a cone?
flashcard.back: Cone with top cut off parallel to base.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The frustum is the lower part after parallel cut.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A frustum of a pyramid has base areas \(20\text{ m}^2\) and \(10\text{ m}^2\) and height \(15\text{ m}\). What is its volume?
questions.explanation: \(V = \frac{1}{3} \times 15 \times (20 + 10 + \sqrt{200}) = 5 \times (30 + 14.14) = 5 \times 44.14 = 220.7\text{ m}^3\). Option B would be from \(\frac{1}{3}h(A+A')\) without the geometric mean.

explanations_tiered.definition: Volume of a frustum of a pyramid is the space between its two parallel bases.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Formula includes the geometric mean of the base areas.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Add the two base areas and their geometric mean, multiply by height over 3.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(\sqrt{AA'}\) accounts for the middle section's area scaling.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Derived by subtracting small pyramid from large pyramid using similar figures.

glossary_entry.definition: Space inside a truncated pyramid or cone.

flashcard.front: Volume formula for frustum of pyramid?

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: Surface Area and Volume of Composed Solids
questions.question: A composed solid consists of a cylinder and a frustum of a cone stacked vertically. How is the total surface area calculated if the base of the frustum matches the top of the cylinder?

options.A: Sum of all individual surface areas
options.B: Sum of all areas minus the area of the common face

questions.hint: The joint between solids is internal and not exposed.
questions.explanation: When two solids are joined, the area of the contacting faces should not be counted in total surface area. Option A would double-count the common base.

source_trace.section_anchor: Surface Area and Volume of Composed Solids
source_trace.content_anchor: Total surface area of the flask is LSA of frustum + lower base area of frustum + LSA of cylinder

explanations_tiered.definition: Total surface area of a composed solid is the sum of exposed surface areas of all components.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Hidden faces (where solids join) are not counted.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The joined faces are inside, so you don't paint them.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Calculate each solid's full surface area, then subtract twice the area of the common face (since it's hidden).
explanations_tiered.advanced: In the flask example: frustum's lower base is attached to cylinder's top, so that circle is excluded from total SA.
reasoning_steps: List all faces of each solid.
Identify faces that are joined (not exposed).
misconceptions: Summing all individual surface areas (Option A) double-counts the common face.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Composed solid SA = sum of exposed faces only.

glossary_entry.definition: The total area of all external surfaces of a solid made of simpler parts.

flashcard.front: How to find surface area of joined solids?
flashcard.back: Sum individual areas, subtract common face twice.

explanations_tiered.workbook_section: Surface Area and Volume of Composed Solids
explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Subtract common face area to avoid double-counting.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: Surface Area and Volume of Composed Solids
questions.question: A cylinder of radius \(10\text{ cm}\) and height \(12\text{ cm}\) has a triangular prism hole (right triangle legs \(3\text{ cm}\) and \(4\text{ cm}\)) drilled through. What is the remaining surface area? (Use \(\pi = 3.14\))
questions.hint: TSA = cylinder LSA + prism LSA + cylinder base area − triangle area × 2.
questions.explanation: Cylinder LSA = \(2\pi(10)(12) = 240\pi = 753.6\) cm². Cylinder base area = \(\pi(100) = 314\) cm². Prism LSA = \(p \times h = (3+4+5) \times 12 = 12 \times 12 = 144\) cm². Subtract triangle area twice (top and bottom holes): \(2 \times 6 = 12\) cm². Total = \(753.6 + 314 + 144 - 12 = 1199.6\)? Wait — that doesn't match. Let me use PDF: TSA = \(2\pi r h + p h + 2\pi r^2 - 2(\frac{1}{2}ab) = 240\pi + 144 + 200\pi - 12 = 440\pi + 132 = 440\times3.14 + 132 = 1381.6 + 132 = 1513.6\) cm². Yes.

calculation_steps: Subtract triangle areas (top and bottom): \(2 \times 6 = 12\) cm²

source_trace.section_anchor: Surface Area and Volume of Composed Solids

explanations_tiered.definition: Total surface area of a drilled solid is the original surface area plus the lateral area of the hole, minus the areas removed from the faces.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Add cylinder LSA, cylinder base areas, prism LSA, then subtract triangle areas (top and bottom).
explanations_tiered.beginner: You paint the outside of the cylinder, the inside of the hole, and the ends, but the hole replaces some end material.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The hole adds its own lateral surface but removes the triangle areas from the cylinder's top and bottom.
explanations_tiered.advanced: TSA = LSA(cylinder) + LSA(prism) + 2 × base area(cylinder) − 2 × area(triangle).
worked_solution: Step 2: Cylinder base area = \(\pi(10^2)=314\) cm²; two bases = 628 cm².
Step 3: Prism LSA = perimeter × height = 12 × 12 = 144 cm².
Step 4: Triangle area = ½×3×4=6 cm²; two removed = 12 cm².
misconceptions: Forgetting to subtract the triangle areas from the cylinder bases.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Drilled solid TSA = cylinder SA + prism LSA − 2×hole face area.

glossary_entry.definition: The total external area including the inner surface of the hole.

flashcard.front: TSA of cylinder with triangular hole through it?
flashcard.back: Add cylinder SA and prism LSA, subtract 2×triangle area.

explanations_tiered.workbook_section: Surface Area and Volume of Composed Solids

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: Surface Area and Volume of Sphere
questions.question: A sphere has a radius of \(6\text{ cm}\). What is its volume?
questions.explanation: \(V = \frac{4}{3}\pi (6^3) = \frac{4}{3}\pi (216) = 4 \times 72 \pi = 288\pi \text{ cm}^3\). Option D (864π) results from using \(4\pi r^3\) (forgetting to divide by 3).

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Surface Area and Volume of Sphere

explanations_tiered.definition: Volume of a sphere is the amount of space inside it.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Formula uses the cube of the radius.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A ball's volume grows quickly as radius increases (r³).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Derived by integrating spherical shells or using Cavalieri's principle.
misconceptions: Using \(4\pi r^3\) (Option D) instead of \(\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3\) is a common error.

glossary_entry.definition: The space inside a sphere, given by \(\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3\).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: Surface Area and Volume of Sphere
questions.question: A cross-section of a sphere made by a plane passing through the center is called a:
questions.hint: It has the same radius as the sphere.
questions.explanation: A great circle is the intersection of a sphere with a plane that passes through the sphere's center. Its radius equals the sphere's radius. Option B (small circle) results from a plane not through the center.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Surface Area and Volume of Sphere
source_trace.content_anchor: If the plane passes through the center of a sphere, the cross-section formed is a great circle

explanations_tiered.definition: A great circle is the largest possible circle on a sphere, with the same center and radius as the sphere.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: It divides the sphere into two equal hemispheres.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Like cutting a ball exactly through the middle — the cut edge is a great circle.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: All great circles of a sphere have the same radius (the sphere's radius).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The equator of Earth is a great circle; lines of longitude are half-great circles.
reasoning_steps: Recall definition of cross-section through center.
misconceptions: A small circle (Option B) results from a plane not through the center.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Great circle: plane through sphere's center.

glossary_entry.definition: The intersection of a sphere and a plane passing through the sphere's center.

flashcard.back: Cross-section of sphere through its center.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Great circle passes through the center.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: Surface Area and Volume of Sphere
questions.question: What is the name of one half of a sphere divided by a great circle?
questions.explanation: A great circle divides a sphere into two equal halves, each called a hemisphere. Option B (great circle) is the dividing line, not the half.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Surface Area and Volume of Sphere
source_trace.content_anchor: A great circle bisects the surface of a sphere. One of the two equal parts is called a hemisphere

explanations_tiered.definition: A hemisphere is half of a sphere, cut by a great circle.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: It has a flat circular face (the great circle) and a curved surface.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Like a dome or half a ball.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Surface area of hemisphere = curved area + base area = \(2\pi r^2 + \pi r^2 = 3\pi r^2\).
reasoning_steps: Recall that a great circle splits the sphere.
misconceptions: Great circle (Option B) is the dividing line, not the half.

glossary_entry.definition: Half of a sphere, including the great circle as its base.

flashcard.back: Half of a sphere divided by a great circle.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Hemisphere means half a sphere.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: Surface Area of Pyramids and Cones
questions.question: A right pyramid has a rectangular base \(4\text{ cm}\) by \(2\text{ cm}\) and altitude \(3\text{ cm}\). What is its lateral surface area?
questions.hint: Find slant heights for both pairs of faces separately.
questions.explanation: For length side 4 cm: slant height \(l_1 = \sqrt{3^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{10}\). Area = \(\frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times \sqrt{10} = 2\sqrt{10}\) (2 faces = \(4\sqrt{10}\)). For width side 2 cm: \(l_2 = \sqrt{3^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{13}\). Area = \(\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times \sqrt{13} = \sqrt{13}\) (2 faces = \(2\sqrt{13}\)). Total LSA = \(2\sqrt{13} + 4\sqrt{10} = 2(\sqrt{13} + 2\sqrt{10})\). Option B has the terms reversed incorrectly.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Surface Area of Pyramids and Cones

explanations_tiered.definition: Lateral surface area of a pyramid is the sum of the areas of its triangular faces.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For rectangular base, two pairs of congruent faces have different slant heights.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Calculate each triangle area separately, then add.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Use Pythagoras to find slant heights from altitude and half the base dimensions.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The slant heights differ because the distance from apex to base edge differs for length and width.
worked_solution: Step 1: Slant height for 2 cm side: \(\sqrt{3^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{13}\).
Step 2: Slant height for 4 cm side: \(\sqrt{3^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{10}\).
Step 3: Area of two 2 cm faces: \(2 \times \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times \sqrt{13} = 2\sqrt{13}\).
Step 4: Area of two 4 cm faces: \(2 \times \frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times \sqrt{10} = 4\sqrt{10}\).
misconceptions: Using the same slant height for all faces is incorrect for non-square bases.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Rectangular pyramid: different slant heights for each face pair.

glossary_entry.term: Lateral surface area of rectangular pyramid
glossary_entry.definition: Sum of areas of four triangular faces, with two distinct slant heights.

flashcard.back: Sum of two face pairs: \(w\cdot l_1 + l\cdot l_2\), where \(l_1, l_2\) are slant heights.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: Surface Area and Volume of Sphere
questions.question: The diameter of an iron ball is \(6\text{ cm}\). What is its surface area? (Use \(\pi = 3.14\))
questions.explanation: Radius \(r = 3\text{ cm}\). SA = \(4 \times 3.14 \times 3^2 = 12.56 \times 9 = 113.04\text{ cm}^2\). Option B (226.08) would be \(4\pi r^2\) with \(r=6\) (using diameter as radius).

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Surface Area and Volume of Sphere

explanations_tiered.definition: Surface area of a sphere is the total area of its outer surface.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Formula uses the square of the radius.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The outside area of a ball, like the leather on a football.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Derived by integrating circumference over angle: \(\int_0^\pi 2\pi r \sin \theta \cdot r d\theta = 4\pi r^2\).

formula_analysis.name: Surface area of sphere
formula_analysis.derivation: Archimedes: sphere area = area of circumscribing cylinder lateral surface.

misconceptions: Using diameter directly as radius (Option B).

glossary_entry.definition: The total area of the curved surface of a sphere.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: The lateral surface area of a frustum of a regular pyramid is \(\frac{1}{2} l (p + p')\). What does \(p\) represent?
questions.hint: It is the larger base's perimeter.
questions.explanation: In the frustum LSA formula, \(p\) is the perimeter of the lower (larger) base, and \(p'\) is the perimeter of the upper (smaller) base. Option A is \(p'\), not \(p\).

source_trace.content_anchor: LSA = ½ l (p + p′), where p is perimeter of lower base, p′ is perimeter of upper base

explanations_tiered.definition: Perimeter of the lower base of a frustum is the sum of all its side lengths.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The frustum has two bases: lower (larger) and upper (smaller).
explanations_tiered.beginner: The distance around the bottom of the frustum.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In LSA formula, \(p\) is the lower base perimeter, \(p'\) is the upper base perimeter.
explanations_tiered.advanced: For a regular frustum, \(p = n \times s_{\text{lower}}\), \(p' = n \times s_{\text{upper}}\).
misconceptions: Confusing lower and upper base perimeters (Option A).

glossary_entry.definition: The sum of the side lengths of the larger base of a frustum.

flashcard.front: What is \(p\) in frustum LSA formula \(\frac{1}{2} l (p + p')\)?

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. p is lower base perimeter.
